December 18, 2024 1 hour 19 minutes Seoul, South Korea

That's what this feels like, you know, it's like, you know, in the short time I've been here, it's not like it's going to fucking destroy my, destroy my body. I'm just visiting, but, but it's very noticeable. But all right, so that was my initial arrival in Seoul. And it's funny how things change now that I've been here about two and a half weeks, almost three weeks, where just things that seem relevant just aren't relevant anymore. The garbage situation. Oh, there's people coming. All right, let's put my money where my mouth is as far as my hiking prowess goes because there's some people coming up behind me. And I was waiting for a bit to see if they'd pass me, but they're just going so fucking slow. It's driving me nuts. So let's see if I can podcast as I continue up this mountain. But yeah, the garbage thing could not be less of a big deal at this point. I just don't care. As my visions of Tokyo have drifted away and I've just gotten used to being here, it's just like, I would ever do this, just how it is. No problem. You adjust to acclimate. There's like a little joke about how if you've never been to South Korea, you might be surprised how brusque the clerks can be. And I'd say that's kind of true. That is pretty rough looking. Maybe no one will follow me this way.

That's a good thing. Now, a bad thing. So, the mountain I was on in that last segment with all the exercise equipment. That's actually one thing that is like all over the place. If you can find a mountain, you can find some exercise equipment. There's all kinds are down by the river. There's like a lot of public exercise stuff, which is pretty cool. If you could actually breathe the air, it would be a lot cooler. But apparently in the summer things are a lot less bad in that sense, so maybe that's really cool. But I noticed a lot of stray cats. Kind of all over, but on that mountain in particular, I noticed a lot. And, you know, people would leave out food and, uh, little umbrellas for shelter that were clearly for the cats. Leave out water. And I saw one cat who, when it saw me, it went and, like, disappeared into a little cave in the rocks at this, on the top of this mountain. And I was like, man, that's unusual. Like, I've never seen cats that skittish around people, even outdoor cats. Like, this thing really was like, you're not getting anywhere near me. And I've never seen a cat hide in a cave. The whole thing was quite like, huh. And I was really just starting to realize just how many cats there were around. And then I got a little turned around on that mountain, but not very much. Just a little bit off the main course. Where I found one of the, uh, the little exercise yards that really wasn't getting used. And then I somehow got a little off the trail. I ended up having to sort of brute force my way down this mountain, but it's quite a small mountain. And that's obviously nothing new for me. So I'm not saying that if you come to Seoul, you're going to see a dead cat because you probably won't. That's probably a me thing. You know, it's just because I was on this weird part of the mountain that people aren't normally on. I got a little lost, but I really was not at all far from the main path. And yeah, I saw this cat laying there and it took a minute where I'm like, there's something very eerie about how that cat's laying there. And yeah, I just watched it for a bit. And it's like, yeah, it's not, it's not getting up. That is a dead cat.

So when I got home, I looked this up. And yeah, it turns out there is a huge problem and has been for decades of stray cats. Basically, there used to be a lot of feral cats in South Korea that you know would just help with pest control and stuff in the more farm type of days. And as the cities have just developed more and more and more, like right now, I'm just looking out over a sea of buildings on this side of this mountain. And there's another sea on the other side of this mountain. There's just nowhere for these cats to go. So there's cats all over the place and apparently locals have taken it upon themselves to catch these cats and pay to have them spayed or neutered. And then they do a little clipping of the ear just so they don't catch the same cat twice just to try to deal with this problem a little bit in a relatively humane way, which is a big step up from how they used to deal with it, which was just that the city would catch them and kill them.

So there's no need to dwell on that point. It's just depressing and creepy and kind of disturbing. And it just made me think for, you know, I just said that weird little feeling for a while after that of like, am I just going to, like any time I'm a little bit off the beaten trail? Am I just going to find a cat that has died of exposure? And I haven't. And again, I think that was kind of even with the number of stray cats here. I think that was, that's not something that's going to happen. That was just weird bad luck, but it was creepy and sad.

But again, like I was saying, these bad parts, you know, the main bad parts being the general level of garbage and stray cats to the point of finding a dead one. Like that's not really, these are kind of, I'd say, ultimately not really relevant to whether I would recommend solar, not. You know, it's like, yeah, it doesn't, it doesn't really affect. It's not really, you know, the garbage is just something you get used to. And it's actually probably still better than a lot of big cities. I'm just, just having come from Japan. It just seemed bad. The cat situation is very sad. But I think that's just because I wander to weird places all the time. And you're really kind of teetering. It's like, well, I like this. But then that's weird. And then there's this. So I went to Muak J Station, MUAK, J-A-E. Apologies if any of my pronunciations are wrong. I really have no idea. I'm just pronouncing stuff phonetically. I don't know how Koreans meant to be pronounced. And then the hiking trail is the in Wonson Mountain Hiking Trail, INWANG SON. And I made a whole little day of it that was like, you know, it's that mix of shallowness and nature that I like when I can do both things. The money is a little confusing because you have to move the decimal point three places. So especially you get these coins, and it's like what am I supposed to do with these coins? They're so little and they're just worth nothing. But the cool thing about small coins is it allows for small transactions. So I found these coffee machines that are in a lot of the subway stations, where for a 500 WON coin, which is about 50 cents Canadian, you can get a little coffee. And you know, I've talked all the time on this podcast about how I love getting those coffees out of little cups from the airports and stuff. But this was just like a tiny one, a little 50 cent version of that, a little tiny, little paper cup with just some strong coffee. You can get various types or you can get Korean tea and just different things for 40 to 50 cents. So it's awesome. It's like everything that I love. It's a little blast of coffee out of a little machine, which is just fun. It's small, so it's not going to make me pee. So that's a bonus. It's just just a blast of caffeine without a lot of extra liquid. And I'm getting rid of a coin that I don't want, that I don't know how else to get rid of. It's win, win, win. It's so, so just fun. Just a fun little thing.

Hmm, I believe this is the mineral water, but it's not like a natural spring. It's like coming out of these big, there's these big vats and you can turn the little spigot. But then it does have a sign that says not drinking water. And this is just a, yet again, just a thing I don't understand. I don't actually know what mineral water is or what it does. Do you rub it on your skin or something? I don't know what it's for. Apparently not drinking. So anyway, I'm halfway down the mountain. I found these. I guess I'm just going to go back up now because if I keep going down, I'll just be down in the city. So back up I go.

But yeah, not only could I get the little 50 cent coffee on the subway and just that I was figuring out how the subway worked and I got my little subway card and that all seems pretty, pretty doable. And then if I went one station over actually from from that station to Hongji station, H-O-N-G-J-E, there's a McDonald's there and I have done some McDonald's and some Burger King while I'm here just because you can just go to the little ordering machine and stick your credit card in and that's it, you know. So as much as I don't mind talking to people here, I still haven't gone into like a proper restaurant or anything. It's still just convenience store stuff. And you can still kind of get some semi-interesting stuff. There's things here that they don't normally have, you know, like bulgogi burgers and spicy chicken things and, you know, man, Korean people love spicy stuff, holy crap. Everything is spicy or sugary or both.

Man, it's weird looking out at the city, just the endless gray of it and then the gray and the sky and how it all just disappears into grayness again because of the pollution which I'll get into, imminently. It's fucking weird looking and almost looks like a weird little fire except it's a perfect rectangle.

But anyway, yeah, hopping on the subway, getting my little 50 cent coffee and then at the McDonald's, I don't know if this is at all Korea specific but I've never seen it before in North America. They have these little baked pies that are like a chocolate pastry on the outside of the pie and inside is raspberry and cream cheese for like $1.80 or something. They're so fucking tasty. So I got my little coffee, I got my little raspberry cream cheese thing from McDonald's. I went over to the hiking trail and I started going up. It's actually the one I was just on as I started de-amoring for this segment. And you know, I'm going up the side of it and it seemed pretty nice. I only saw a couple of people, not that many. There were stairs. Lots of stairs built into the mountains. Like it's a very easy, really well developed. Lots of signage too. And I was like, you know, this isn't so bad. This has been kind of a fun day and this little weird hike seems all right. And then I got to the top and is way, way, way better than I ever expected. I expected, you know, a bit of just a mountain. Just a kind of whatever. I'm just glad I can go up the mountain. I didn't expect there to be much up here. But then at the top is a wall. Is like the great fortress wall or something it's called. I'd never heard of this but now I know all about it. Apparently it was first built in 13 something and was very, very long helped repel invaders and whatnot throughout history.

And then the most recent major event was in 1968. There was an attempt at an assassination by North Korea on whoever was the current leader of South Korea. That they repelled, but after that they put a bunch of guard posts up on this mountain along this wall, what remains of the wall. Mostly the wall seems only really remains on this mountain, I think. And then in 2018 they finally decommissioned them all. I'm like, okay, we don't really need this anymore. So there's a couple ornamental ones left. There's like a weird little reading room up here that you can just go hang out in. But it has no washroom. So it didn't do me much good. But it was an old guard outpost. It's kind of cool that it's still here. So this wall, it's like a little version of the Great Wall of China kind of thing. And it's just this wall that goes along the ridge of the mountain. And there's a bunch of people up here and again it's all well developed and lots of anywhere that's a little steep or dangerous. There's fences put up, lots of signage to help you go through the various ways off the mountain. The way I came up is kind of weird. I think you're supposed to just start at one end of the wall and walk along the wall. But I ended up walking along the wall down. I went northwest that time. So today I'm going southeast to go the other way on the wall. And yeah, you get this amazing view of the city from up here too. It's just like man on either side you can just see these incredible views. And it's like this is this is a plus. Again, my ultimate recommendation is going to be do not come to Seoul, especially in the winter.

But if you are here, definitely this is worth it. I mean, this is an A plus thing to see. This is really, really cool. So I was thinking like, man, maybe this is all right. Maybe this is all turning out okay. 1:00:00 Maybe I can just come up here every day or do stuff like this because then from here there's other bigger mountains in the distance that also have little hiking trail markers and signs pointing to them. I don't quite know how that's all going to go, but even if that goes nowhere, even if it's just this. This is great. This is really, really cool.

So that's the next good thing. This is my favorite thing, as I was saying in Japan. When you have access to a mountain hike that is very close to a city, a city adjacent, that's just my favorite thing. It's so nice and convenient to get here. I was on another smaller mountain yesterday to take a little test hike and test out these masks. And it was also just really well-signed-posted. Lots of stairs. These are for real stairs. Wooden stairs. You know, there's a certain charm to a lot of the hiking in Japan where you're literally just on the ridge of a mountain on a trail. But this is really cool too. This is so set up.

It's set up to come to the mountain. Like, anybody could do it. It's no problem. And there's so many different vantage points and places you can get views of the city. It's really, really fucking good, which is what makes the final down. Such a super downer. Because it just destroys all of that. It destroys everything. I was thinking, could I name this podcast, Learning to Love Soul? And right away I was like, no, that's too much. I do not love soul. I'm afraid. There's too much weird shit going on around here. It is full of garbage and dead cats. It would be disingenuous for me to say that I love it because I just don't. But I was like, yeah, but I'll call it Learning to Like Soul because I'm really starting to like this. This is starting to be okay.

But then the final super mega downer was that from up here, from seeing these views. That's when the extent of the pollution became so evident. Because looking out at these views of the city, and it was like this yesterday, it was like this the day before. It wasn't just a coincidence. It wasn't just a bad day. It legitimately looks like the road. Kormac McCarthy's the road. I'm not fucking kidding. That's what it looks like. You know, obviously the dust level is not that severe. It's a tenth of that. It's not apocalypse level. But it's so much closer to a apocalypse level than I've ever seen with my own eyes, not in a movie, or not in a video game. It's fucking disturbing. It is just gray.

Just gray. I mean, I don't need any other words. Everything just dissolves into gray. Right now today, not a cloudy day. Just gray. Just grayness. The distance just disappears into gray. In every direction. It's fucking creepy. And that's where I knew pretty much right away I knew something weird was going on. I was definitely smelling weird, a weird odor in the city. That was the first thing I noticed. And then just noticing just an uncomfortable feeling in my lungs. And I did a little teeny cursory research and just saw that someone said like, oh yeah, it is a pollution problem here. So that's when I started wearing my little cloth mask and that just made me feel a little better. But when you're down in the city, I guess just because you don't have the ability to see that far.

It's harder to tell how bad things are, although you can still very clearly tell. Just look at the sun. And you know, you can't see it. It's just a weird haze.

It's clear something is wrong, but I did not understand the extent of how fucking bad things are until I got to the top of this mountain. And it's like, yeah, technically, these are amazing views. Technically, this is fucking wonderful.

But what I'm looking at is terrifying. It's fucked up. It is just fucked up.

And yeah, because I spent the day hiking and breathing deeply, I got to do a little more research about how I realized in Japan that at about 500 meters. That's when I really start feeling uncomfortable. So the peak of this mountain in one sun, I was feeling a little, a little weird. Like not great, but not like scared scared, just like a little bit like, oh, I'm feeling that weird little rubberyness just a little bit in my legs. 1:05:00 So if 500 is where I really start feeling bad, I wonder what this is. And when I got to the peak, it was just below 400 meters. And I'm like, yeah, that checks out. That seems about right. You know, not enough to turn me away, but definitely get into the point that I'm like, ooh, a little bit high up here.

But yeah, high enough to see everything all around. Just fucking beautiful. But at the same time, just the worst thing you've ever seen in your life.

If Japan was like a dream, I don't know how I can describe Seoul other than a nightmare. You know, that might sound fucking like I'm overstating it. But a stress dream at the very least. It's like, this is not fucking okay.

And as I researched it, I mean, I just feel bad for this city. I feel bad for Seoul because it's not their fault necessarily. Like, it's hard to determine exactly where all the pollution comes from. It's definitely the geography of the mountains that traps it here.

And apparently in winter, the cold weather somehow like pushes it down. And that's why it's so much worse in the winter. I'm here at the worst possible time apparently. But they have a hard time determining exactly where all this stuff comes from. Some of it is definitely domestic. Maybe up to half, like they're really not sure. But there is a lot of like, coal energy and stuff going on here that's not working out. But they're working on it. They're trying to reduce it. But a lot of it does just come in from the yellow dust from these various deserts, but then just the pollutants from China. And that's just such a raw deal. It's like, it's not even their fault. They're just paying for the sins of China. And man, are they paying? I mean, I'm looking at it right now. It's like, I just can't believe this is real. It looks like a fucking Photoshop job where you just take the contrast and turn it away, fucking down. So everything just has a blanket of grainess across it.

And yeah, the buildings and the mountains are disappearing into the distance, but not because of distance because you can't fucking see. Because it's just pollution everywhere. It's so fucked up.

So yeah, I mean, I don't know what to say besides I just have, again, if a city is a person, if you treat a city like a person, I have great pity for this city. It just is like fucking terrible the situation that they're in. And I don't know how they're going to get out of it. Because again, like, apparently they are slowly working on fixing their end of the pollution. But the rest of the world is just going to keep dumping its fucking shit on this city.

And there's all this beautifulness of these mountains and stuff that is so cool.

But every view is just a view of fucking hell, man. It's just fucking awful.

So yeah, as I researched more, that's when I learned about that, that you need the specific masks to block the really tiny particulates. And luckily, they're really cheap. I mean, I got like 15 of these masks for like five bucks at Daiso. So it's like 33 cents a day on masks. That's no problem. I could pay for that.

And so far, I definitely feel better. Although yesterday was a smaller hike, and I felt fine today. I mean, I don't know. I do feel like my throat hurts a little bit, but I don't know if it's psychosomatic. I don't know if it's real. Definitely the mask is helping, but I mean, I probably just shouldn't be doing this. Because that's the other thing I learned about is the AQI, the air quality index that you're supposed to check every day. And yeah, that day that I hiked up here without a proper mask, it was red, blazing red, which is like, don't do it, man. Don't go outside. Don't do physical activity.

Today, it was only moderate, which is like, I don't know. I certainly don't feel great about it. And I mean, I'm still I'm looking out of the city. Doesn't look any different today. I have a feeling when I get home and check it will probably be back to the extreme red level, because it seems to go up during the day. It'll say, okay, in the morning, then worse than worse than worse. And yeah, I'm sure this is not unique to this city, but this is never something I'd ever even really been aware of. I'd never had to check the air pollutant level before I go outside. I just never even knew this was a thing, really. I guess I've heard about polluted cities, but I've never been to one. It's a little shocking to me.

And just such an utter deal breaker. So that's why I say, like, don't come in the winter. Do not come. Not worth it.

I want to try elsewhere. It does seem like soul, not only because of the location, but because of the size, is by far the worst for this. So I was looking into train to Busan, classic train to Busan. I can get there for like a hundred bucks.

I'm pretty sure that sometimes happens. And I got it in my head. I'm like, you know, if I can, I'm going to cancel the other half. Why the fuck would I stay here? Let me get on that train to Busan. Let me get the fuck out of here. Fight the zombies. Get to the other side.

But unfortunately, the place I booked, it's only a 50% return. And it's like, oh, well, that's, I mean, I got masks now. At least I know what's going on. I'm only here for five weeks. It'll be fine. I'll make this work. But yeah, I wanted to, I wanted to leave as soon as it occurred to me like, hey, I could get out of here. I was like, yeah, why wouldn't I? Why the fuck would I stay here? This is so insane. And it's such a shame. I mean, yeah, I'm up by the wall now. And I mean, this view would be one of the best views ever. And instead, it's just like scary and weird.

And again, I don't mean to criticize, you know, sorry if it sounds like I'm being mean. I know it's not your fault, Saul. I feel bad for you. I have great sympathy for this situation you're in. But I don't have to fucking deal with this. This is insane. You know, I can't imagine why I would ever come back unless it really is way, way better in the summer.

Hopefully, hopefully I can make South Korea part of my little, you know, travel adventures. But I will be heading straight to some other city. There's no question about that.

And at least adults, you know, they have options. They made their choice. They want to live in the big city. They want to put up with this in the winter. Maybe it does clear up other times a year. You want to mask, just deal with it. I don't know, just cut 10 years off your life and just accept that. I don't know, whatever your justification is. But the other day, sitting at the window with the McDonald's eating my little raspberry pie. And a lady went by with her kid, like this tiny little kid wearing the mask. And man, it made me feel so bad because it's like, imagine being a kid who has to grow up here and wear your little mask because you live in the metro games. You live in the road. You live in the fucking apocalypse. That's going to say about the road. They never really say in the road specifically what happened to what type of apocalypse it is. There's something through so much dust in the air that it blocked out the sun and that was enough to just destroy the world. And that's fucking exactly what it's like here. It's just gray everywhere. And yeah, like I said, it's not as much as the road, but if you just take the road and just turn it down.

Or if you take this and turn it up, you know, it's the exact same vibe, exact same look, just, just less.

I mean, I suppose like I had no idea what I was walking into. I had no idea it was going to be like this or sure as fuck would not have booked two months. I would have booked two fucking days here, you know, just to sort of situate myself and get transit sorted out and go somewhere else. Or I mean, presumably there's flights straight to Busan or whatever. You could just forgo this entirely.

But yeah, I suppose if you knew what you're getting into, if you're, if you want to experience apocalypse tourism. If you want to see what the fucking apocalypse looks like, then yeah, okay, you can do worse, man. Come to Seoul, get some fucking K94 masks.

Go hike up a mountain and just look at what hell looks like. Look at the fucking horrifying future. I mean, it's worse than Fallout. Fallout doesn't look like this. The game Fallout. Yeah, it's the equivalent of smoking some cigarettes, but you know, that doesn't kill you right away. But yeah, I don't know. I just don't know what to say except don't come here in the winter.
